metadata.source: Second audit pass: conservative mushroom safety and flag corrections

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when thoroughly cooked. Keep the strong warning emphasis on confusion with false morels; this is a look-alike caution issue, not a special preparation tag.
Choice edible when thoroughly cooked. The main caution is confusion with false morels, not unusual preparation beyond normal cooking.
Choice edible when thoroughly cooked. Keep the caution focused on false-morel confusion, not on treating normal cooking as special prep.
Danger group. Do not treat false morels like true morels. This is a poisoning risk entry, not a prep candidate.
Edible when young and tender, but some people react poorly and older specimens get tough. Treat this as a caution / tolerance issue rather than a special-prep mushroom.
Edible when correctly identified, but confusion with toxic Agaricus and white Amanitas is serious. Caution belongs on the ID side, not the prep side.
Edible when correctly identified, but this is a strong caution entry because of deadly Amanita confusion and toxic Agaricus look-alikes.
Choice edible bolete, but beginners still need caution because porcini-like boletes have important look-alikes and confusing group boundaries.
Only edible while the interior is pure white and uniform. That is a harvest-stage rule, not a prep tag.
Edible when young and still white before deliquescing. This is a harvest-stage timing rule, not a special-prep tag.
Edible with caution. This one legitimately keeps the prep flag because many foragers peel the slimy cap cuticle before cooking.
Choice edible, but this one legitimately keeps prep attention because it often needs especially careful cleaning through the folds and branches.
Good edible mushroom, but keep caution because this entry is under review for whether it belongs as a true wild Upper Michigan species instead of mainly cultivated context.
Edible when correctly identified and cooked. Keep the caution on look-alikes and faded old specimens rather than calling normal cooking special prep.
Edible when correctly identified, but the real issue is deadly Galerina confusion. Keep the warning as an identification caution, not a prep rule.
Not poisonous in the classic sense, but strongly bitter and not for eating. Keep it as a caution / spoiler entry, not an edible one.
Edible only when young and tender. Treat that as an age/harvest-stage rule, not as a special-prep tag beyond normal cooking.
Choice edible only when the classic firm parasitized form is present and in good condition. This is a strong identification and condition caution entry.
This one legitimately keeps both caution and prep attention because reactions are common and thorough cooking plus a small first trial matters more than for ordinary edible mushrooms.
Only the classic firm white aborted form is considered edible by experienced foragers. Treat this as a strong condition and identification caution entry, not a normal edible mushroom.
